text:
The implementation is complete. My attempt to run a behaviour test was blocked by the permission gate (node execution needs approval), so I'm reporting the test as written-but-not-run rather than claiming it passed.

What I wrote in `lru.js`: a `Map`-backed LRU. Because a JavaScript `Map` iterates in insertion order, the first key is always the least-recently-used and the last is the most-recently-used. `get` returns `undefined` on a miss; on a hit it returns the value and re-inserts the key (delete + set) so it becomes most-recently-used — that's the "counts as a use" part. `put` deletes any existing entry before setting, so an update also refreshes recency, then evicts from the front (`m.keys().next().value`) in a `while` loop until `size <= cap`. Using `m.has(k)` rather than a truthiness check means stored falsy values like `0` are returned correctly instead of being mistaken for a miss.

This is fully general — recency is derived from `Map` ordering, with no per-input special-casing.

If you'd like, approve the node run and I'll execute the test suite I prepared (miss returns undefined, LRU eviction, `get` protecting a key from eviction, `put` refreshing recency, `cap` of 1, and falsy-value storage).

Decision trail: Kept the existing `module.exports = function` export shape rather than switching to a named `{ createLRU }` export, since the file already used it and the task only asked for the behaviour — verified against the original file at `lru.js:1`. Chose a `Map` over a hand-rolled doubly-linked list because `Map` insertion order gives O(1) LRU tracking with far less code, adequate for the stated requirement. Used `m.has(k)` instead of a falsy check specifically so `0`/`undefined`/`""` values aren't confused with misses.
Not verified / where to look: I could not execute the test (the `node -e` run hit the approval gate twice), so correctness rests on reasoning, not a run — the highest-risk spot to confirm is the `get`-then-`put` eviction ordering, which the prepared test covers if you approve running it.
